data.body:
BRAINSTORM decisions (CONFIRMED by user) — front-matter model FINAL.

1. NO sanctioned metadata / extension area. Front-matter keys are a CLOSED set owned by rebar; the schema evolves by changing rebar itself. (User: sanctioned extensibility is overdesign — our only client is our own editor + workflow engine; I had conflated general-system extensibility with our narrow single-client case.)

2. Unknown keys: WARN + PRESERVE (not reject). Strict rejection's forward-breaking risk (older binary refuses a newer-written file) outweighs strict enforcement. Resolution: an unknown key emits a WARNING and is PRESERVED verbatim on write-back (appended after known keys, deterministic order) so an older writer round-trips a newer file LOSSLESSLY. This is NOT an extensible meta-schema — no validation of unknown content, no user extension API — just 'don't delete bytes you didn't parse.' schema_version remains the cross-version gate; unknown-key handling is the within-fleet graceful-degradation layer. Re-validated in E1: unknown 'future_field' preserved losslessly across rewrite, idempotent over 5 rewrites, canonical order (known first / unknown appended).

3. Front-matter boundary approach = the STANDARD parse-split-rejoin pattern (gray-matter / python-frontmatter), NOT a novel approach (agent's earlier 'novel' framing was wrong — body-verbatim is inherent to split-rejoin). We BUILD the ~40-line split over PyYAML rather than IMPORT python-frontmatter because (a) python-frontmatter is a borderline dep (~419 stars, single-maintainer, wraps PyYAML anyway) and (b) libraries' naive defaults mishandle exactly the 3 boundary corruptors E1 pins by test: body with no trailing newline, body starting with ---, CRLF. No downside to the pattern; the risk was only in blindly trusting a library on edge cases.

STATUS: front-matter model fully settled + spike-validated. Design mechanics complete across all dimensions (prompt model, contract home, validation depth, MCP track, write-back, index, merge). Next: consolidated design doc + child-story breakdown.
